evidence: Large callback-bag runtime structs are repeated across core flows: `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` (many `Start*Fn` fields), `importerRuntime`/embedded sub-runtimes in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, and `workflowRuntime` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`.

Broadcast state path adds multiple pass-through layers (`construct*WithRuntime`, `broadcast*CollectorContract`, closure indirection in `broadcastLoopRuntime`) without policy/translation being added in `goseg/broadcast/state_lifecycle.go` and `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`.

Config update abstraction mirrors schema via a very large hand-bound registry (`registryConfigPatchFields`) in `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go`, tightly coupled to `structs.SysConfig` sections in `goseg/structs/configs.go`.

Feature modules depend on broad orchestration dependency bundles instead of narrow contracts: `importerProvisionRuntime` embeds `dockerOrchestration.RuntimeContainerOps` and `RuntimeVolumeOps` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, while `workflowRuntime` embeds `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, and `RuntimeSnapshotOps` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` centralizes many dependency groups and exports them widely, increasing transitive coupling pressure for downstream modules.

`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` directly wires many subsystem dependencies (`subsystem`, `leak`, `rectify`, `routines`, `logstream`) in one runtime default constructor, making startup a high-coupling hub.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows `goseg/protocol/contracts/` at 11 files (mixed protocol + StarTram contract families in one folder), crossing the >10 mixed-concern split signal in the packet guidance.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` defines upload-domain contract IDs and governance owner `uploadsvc`, while upload action behavior checks live in `goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go`, indicating one concern split across directories.

Contract conformance validation for upload actions is placed in `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go` while upload action parsing tests are in `goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go`, creating duplicated compatibility-surface ownership across package boundaries.
Root `README.md` embeds subsystem-specific backend quality gate commands and governance links, making root documentation a policy hub instead of delegating to subsystem-local governance docs.

Runtime wiring is implemented as large hand-written seam bundles with many near-identical constructor/default functions in goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go (e.g., Runtime*Ops structs at lines 17-137 and default builders at 175-350), increasing structural noise and change surface.

Compatibility and indirection layers are partly pass-through mirrors (goseg/system/disk/adapter.go lines 11-61), while other files keep separate mirrored view structs for same data (goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go lines 17-63 and 65-89), indicating repeated manual synchronization patterns.

Single files carry multiple operational concerns: goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go combines alias policy, minio link orchestration, runtime config mutation, container restart logic, and schedule publishing (lines 38-541).

Several tests bundle many behaviors in one body instead of focused scenario units (e.g., goseg/handler/systemsvc/system_test.go lines 141-229 and goseg/config/config_test.go lines 72-185), which mirrors and reinforces mixed-responsibility production design.

findings.summary: Runtime callback-bag pattern is over-expanded, creating high-indirection orchestration surfaces.
evidence: `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` defines many service-specific callbacks and a parallel service-spec mapping, then separately validates/wires them.
`importerRuntime` is split into multiple embedded runtime structs with many callback fields plus scope-specific resolvers/validators (`resolveImporterRuntimeFor*`).
`workflowRuntime` embeds multiple orchestration op bundles and repeatedly resolves runtime before operations.
findings.suggestion: Introduce smaller domain-specific runtime interfaces (for example: transition bus, container lifecycle, config IO) and compose them at module boundaries; replace monolithic callback bags with 3-5 focused contracts reused across startup/importer/workflow.

findings.summary: Broadcast runtime path contains pass-through wrappers that add call depth without behavior.
evidence: `constructPierInfoWithRuntime`, `constructAppsInfoWithRuntime`, `constructProfileInfoWithRuntime`, and `constructSystemInfoWithRuntime` are thin pass-throughs to collector contracts.
`newBroadcastLoopRuntimeWithController` wraps these pass-throughs again in closures before tick execution.
The state bootstrap/tick flow bounces through helper indirection (`construct*` + closure fields) before doing the same collector calls.
findings.suggestion: Collapse the wrapper chain by injecting a single typed collector bundle into the loop/state lifecycle and calling collector methods directly in tick/bootstrap paths; keep only wrappers that enforce policy or translate data.

findings.summary: Config patch registry mirrors config schema with large manual bindings, reducing abstraction payoff.
evidence: `registryConfigPatchFields` contains a long per-field binding list mapping key/patch/config accessors and merge behavior.
The registry depends on manual alignment with `SysConfig` section fields and patch fields, which duplicates schema knowledge in multiple places.
Small schema evolution requires touching multiple repetitive binding entries, increasing drift risk.
findings.suggestion: Move to section-level patch handlers with generated/declared metadata per section (connectivity/runtime/startram/etc.) so field mapping and merge policy are co-located with each section definition, not centralized in one large registry function.

findings.summary: Modules consume oversized orchestration dependency bundles, creating avoidable coupling.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` exports many grouped runtime op structs and default builders, becoming a central dependency source.
`importer` and `shipworkflow` embed broad orchestration op structs instead of narrow per-use interfaces.
Startup default wiring references many subsystem entrypoints directly, concentrating cross-package dependencies in one orchestration hub.
findings.suggestion: Define narrow consumer-owned interfaces in importer/workflow/startup (only required methods), then adapt orchestration runtime bundles behind adapters to reduce transitive dependency exposure and boundary churn.

findings.summary: Top-level shipworkflow package mixes lifecycle domain logic with infrastructure provisioning flows.
evidence: Directory profile for goseg/shipworkflow/ is 48 files, with broad mixed responsibilities in one folder.
newship.go imports and orchestrates config, docker/events, docker/network, docker/orchestration, shipcleanup, shipcreator, and startram directly.
lifecycle.go contains a narrower lifecycle wait primitive while provisioning/runtime choreography lives alongside it in the same root package.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/shipworkflow/provisioning/` and move provisioning-heavy flows (`newship.go`) there; (2) create `goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle/` and move lifecycle wait/state helpers (`lifecycle.go`) there; (3) keep `goseg/shipworkflow` root as thin facade/orchestrator entrypoints. Move order: lifecycle helpers first, then provisioning flows, then update facade calls. After each move run `rg -n "shipworkflow\.|provisioning\.|lifecycle\." goseg -g '*.go'` to update references, `gofmt -w goseg/shipworkflow/**/*.go`, then validate with `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/... ./goseg/api/...`.

findings.summary: Config root directory is overloaded, obscuring clear ownership boundaries across runtime, bootstrap, and state.
evidence: holistic directory profile lists goseg/config/ at 41 files and 5474 LOC, indicating a flat mixed-concern directory.
bootstrap.go handles startup/initialization sequencing and filesystem bootstrapping while config.go contains runtime context accessors and startram snapshot state APIs.
Tests mirror this mixed surface in the same package root rather than package-specific test boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) introduce `goseg/config/bootstrap/` for initialization and path/debug boot logic; (2) introduce `goseg/config/runtime/` for runtime context getters/setters; (3) introduce `goseg/config/startramstate/` for startram snapshot/cache access. Move order: extract runtime context wrappers first (lowest risk), then startram state accessors, then bootstrap initialization entrypoints with a thin root-level compatibility facade if needed. Update imports with `rg -n "groundseg/config" goseg -g '*.go'`, apply `gofmt -w goseg/config/**/*.go`, then run `go test ./goseg/config/...` and `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Disk compatibility shim API is exposed at system root instead of a disk-scoped boundary.
evidence: disk_shims.go is mostly pass-through wrappers to `storagefacade` and `system/disk` types, but remains in package `system`.
disk_test.go and disk_storage_test.go validate disk behaviors through top-level `system` symbols (`CreateGroundSegFilesystem`, `ListHardDisks`, `parseFstabLine`, etc.), reinforcing a broad root API.
holistic directory profile for goseg/system/ shows 35 files in one root directory, indicating broad concern mixing.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/system/diskcompat/` (or expand `goseg/system/disk/`) and move shim wrappers there; (2) keep temporary root-level forwarding functions in `system` with deprecation comments; (3) migrate tests and call sites to the disk-scoped package, then remove root forwards. Move order: add new package + duplicate wrappers, migrate imports and tests, finally delete root wrappers. Run `rg -n "\b(CreateGroundSegFilesystem|ListHardDisks|parseFstabLine|reconcileFstabLines)\b" goseg -g '*.go'` for import updates, then `gofmt -w goseg/system/**/*.go` and validate via `go test ./goseg/system/...`.

findings.summary: Upload contract ownership is split between protocol/contracts and uploadsvc packages.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` defines `UploadOpenEndpointAction` and `UploadResetAction` with governance owner `uploadsvc` inside `goseg/protocol/contracts`.
`uploadsvc/actions_test.go` validates the same upload action tokens (`open-endpoint`, `reset`) in a separate package.
`conformance/fixtures_test.go` additionally validates upload action-to-contract alignment, so compatibility ownership is distributed across multiple directories.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/uploadsvc/contractspec/` for upload-owned contract specs and binding fixtures; (2) move upload contract spec builders from `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` into that folder, keeping protocol-wide registry in `goseg/protocol/contracts`; (3) move upload-specific conformance assertions from `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go` into `goseg/uploadsvc/contractspec` tests; (4) keep only cross-family validation in `goseg/protocol/contracts`; (5) run `cd goseg && gofmt ./... && go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...` and then `go test ./...` to validate import/path updates.

findings.summary: Protocol and StarTram contract families are co-located in a single contracts folder with mixed ownership.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go` represent distinct families and owners (`uploadsvc/system/wifi` vs `startram`) but live in the same package.
directory profile reports `goseg/protocol/contracts/` has 11 files, matching the flat-directory overload threshold used in the packet guidance for mixed concerns.
README quality gate links multiple governance docs, reinforcing that contract governance is multi-domain rather than a single cohesive package.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) introduce family-scoped folders `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocolfamily/` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/startramfamily/`; (2) move family-specific spec/constants/validators into those folders; (3) keep shared types/registry in `goseg/protocol/contracts`; (4) update imports and exported entrypoints to preserve external API compatibility; (5) validate with `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Root README carries subsystem policy details that should live with owning packages.
evidence: README includes detailed backend quality-gate command sequences and subsystem governance links rather than just navigation pointers.
Contract governance is implemented in package code (`protocol_contracts.go` ownership metadata), but policy execution details are anchored in root docs, increasing docs-vs-runtime boundary spread.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) reduce root `README.md` to high-level entrypoints; (2) move backend quality-gate procedure text to `docs/architecture/backend-quality-gates.md` and package-local governance docs; (3) keep README as link map only; (4) run `rg -n "Backend Quality Gate|protocol/contracts/GOVERNANCE" README.md docs goseg` to verify references and remove stale paths.

findings.summary: Runtime seam construction is fragmented into large hand-wired bundles that mix wiring policy with domain behavior boundaries.
evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go defines many Runtime*Ops structs and default builders (lines 17-137, 175-350) with repetitive callback assignment logic.

goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go consumes broad cross-domain runtime behavior in one command map and handler set (lines 38-63, 393-541), making seam definitions and command orchestration tightly coupled.
goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go duplicates runtime/config/strategy views and normalization assignment chains (lines 17-63, 148-200), extending the same manual-wiring pattern into another subsystem.
findings.suggestion: Introduce per-domain runtime modules with explicit constructor contracts (e.g., `networkRuntime`, `urbitRuntime`, `configRuntime`) and compose them via a small top-level coordinator. Replace repeated field-by-field assignment with generated or table-driven wiring descriptors.

findings.summary: Thin compatibility wrappers are preserved as full files/functions, creating parallel APIs that add little behavior and increase synchronization burden.
evidence: goseg/system/disk/adapter.go is almost entirely pass-through wrappers to `storage` (ParseFstabLine through RemoveMultipartFiles at lines 11-61).
goseg/system/storage/storage_test.go validates behavior directly in `storage`, while adapter-level behavior is not distinct (lines 50-262), indicating duplicated boundary without separate policy.

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go also uses type aliases and forwarding-style wrappers (e.g., `type RuntimeFileOps = container.RuntimeFileOps` at line 94 plus default builders), reinforcing cross-package forwarding instead of clear ownership.
findings.suggestion: Collapse compatibility wrappers behind a single migration boundary: either fully re-export via package-level aliases with deprecation markers, or move callers directly to `storage` and remove forwarder functions in one staged migration.

findings.summary: Urbit transition runtime file mixes multiple independent concerns in one flow layer, making command evolution and failure handling hard to reason about.
evidence: goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go includes command routing, alias checks, startram alias operations, minio link orchestration, config persistence, container lifecycle actions, and schedule publication in the same unit (lines 38-541).
The file relies on broad shared config/embedded models (`structs.UrbitDocker` and multiple section writes) which encourages cross-concern mutation paths (lines 65-126, 429-475).
Runtime dependencies are broad and loosely grouped in orchestration runtime ops (goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go lines 65-74), matching the same concern mixing in execution code.
findings.suggestion: Split transition handling by concern (`alias`, `runtime-config`, `container-lifecycle`, `schedule`) behind a small command dispatcher. Keep each handler with a narrow dependency interface and dedicated error policy strategy.

findings.summary: Protocol/command behavior is asserted through repeated bespoke tests across packages instead of a shared contract harness.
evidence: goseg/protocol/actions/actions_test.go contains overlapping unknown-action checks (`TestParseActionRejectsUnknownAction` lines 102-114 and `TestParseUploadActionRejectsUnknown` lines 195-207).

goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go re-validates canonical C2C action token and envelope parsing behavior (`TestProcessMessageRoutesSupportedAction` lines 13-45 and `TestC2CPayloadUsesCanonicalActionToken` lines 182-195).

goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go repeats action/payload matrix checks with many near-duplicate executor scenarios (lines 90-190, 222-260), overlapping responsibilities already tested in action/contracts packages.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared protocol contract test harness that owns action-token, payload-shape, and dispatch-matrix assertions once; let package tests focus on package-specific behavior (integration wiring/error mapping) only.

findings.summary: Large multi-purpose tests encode many behavioral branches in one function, reducing readability and masking responsibility boundaries.
evidence: goseg/handler/systemsvc/system_test.go `TestHandleSystemSwapWifiAndUpdate` validates swap, update, wifi toggle, wifi connect success/failure in one test body (lines 141-229).

goseg/config/config_test.go `TestMergeConfigsMigrationUsesDefaultsAndGeneratedSaltWhenMissing` sets and verifies a very broad field matrix (lines 72-185), bundling many merge responsibilities together.
goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go `TestCheckParametersValidationPaths` mutates one runtime through many invalid states sequentially (lines 142-195), making failures harder to localize.
findings.suggestion: Split mega-tests into focused, table-driven subtests with one behavior axis per case. Introduce reusable fixture builders so state setup is declarative and each assertion maps to a single responsibility.
